reasoning: The National Heads-Up Poker Championship historically features a field of 64 invited players, meaning the base rate for any average player to win is roughly 1/64 (1.56%). John Smith is a well-known heads-up specialist, but he would be facing a very tough field of top professionals and his participation is not guaranteed. Given the large field size, the high variance of heads-up poker, and the fact that 6 match wins in a row are usually required, the baseline probability for almost any individual player is naturally going to be around 1% to 3%. Because individual players have a very low probability of winning the entire tournament, I am setting the probability to the minimum allowed value of 0.02 to reflect the long odds.
